scenario: FEEL: extract domain from email "user@example.com" -- want "example.com". Team wonders substring before / substring after. FEEL substring before / substring after?
question: FEEL substring before / substring after?

options.text: FEEL provides both substring before(string, match) and substring after(string, match) as standard built-in functions defined in the DMN specification. com", "@") returns "user" (the portion of the string before the first occurrence of "@"). com" (the portion after). If the delimiter is absent from the string, both functions return an empty string. These functions are available in all compliant FEEL engines including Camunda 8 and are the idiomatic way to parse delimited strings in DMN decisions and BPMN expressions.

FEEL provides dedicated string extraction functions: substring before(string, match) returns the part of the string that appears before the first occurrence of the match string, and substring after(string, match) returns the part after it. com" -- the exact domain extraction needed. If the delimiter is absent from the string, both functions return an empty string. These are standard built-in functions defined in the DMN FEEL specification and available in all compliant engines including Camunda 8. They are more readable and semantically clear than manual index arithmetic or split-based alternatives.
Use split -- this is only partially workable. com"]. com", "@")[2] -- using 1-based list indexing to get the second element. com", "@") which directly expresses the intent.

Manual indexing -- this is only partially workable. Manual string index operations in FEEL would involve finding the position of "@" using string length and substring with offsets, which is verbose and error-prone. FEEL provides the higher-level substring before and substring after built-in functions specifically to avoid this. The substring(string, start, length) function works with character positions (1-based in FEEL), but computing the correct start position requires finding the delimiter index first. Using substring after is the correct, readable, and specification-aligned approach.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL substring extraction: substring before(string, match): Returns portion of string before first occurrence of match. `feel substring before("user@example.com", "@") → "user" substring before("foo/bar/baz", "/") → "foo" substring before("hello", "x") → "" (not found) substring after(string, match): Returns portion after first occurrence. feel substring after("user@example.com", "@") → "example.com" substring after("foo/bar/baz", "/") → "bar/baz" Combined: Extract email domain: feel domain: substring after(email, "@") Extract local part: feel localPart: substring before(email, "@") Extract username from URL path: feel path: "/users/alice/posts" username: substring before(substring after(path, "/users/"), "/") Use cases: Email parsing: Username = before "@". Domain = after "@". URL parsing: Scheme = before "://". Host+path = after "://". File extension: Name = before "." (or last "."). Ext = after "." (or last "."). Note on last-occurrence: Standard substring before / after: first occurrence typically. For last occurrence: May need regex or recursive approach. Verify FEEL version's support. Compare with split: feel parts: split("user@example.com", "@") → ["user", "example.com"] Returns list; access via indexing: parts[1] → "user". parts[2] → "example.com". Use split when multiple delimiters or want all parts. Compare with index of (per Set 15 Q49): substring before / after: direct extraction. index of + substring: more general. For nested extraction: Email "user.name@subdomain.example.com": feel localPart: substring before(email, "@") // → "user.name" domain: substring after(email, "@") // → "subdomain.example.com" topLevelDomain: substring after(domain, ".") // first "." // → "example.com" (not "com" -- first . from left) For "last . extraction": more complex; use regex or recursive. For defensive parsing: feel =if contains(email, "@") then substring after(email, "@") else null Avoid errors on malformed input. For multi-step extraction: feel { fullPath: "https://example.com/users/alice", afterScheme: substring after(fullPath, "://"), // "example.com/users/alice" host: substring before(afterScheme, "/"), // "example.com" path: substring after(afterScheme, "/") // "users/alice" } Chained operations. For empty results: Delimiter not found: empty string (""). Defensive: check empty. For regex alternative: feel domain: replace(email, "^.+@", "") Use replace for regex-based extraction; verify FEEL implementation. Best practices: Use substring before/after for simple delimiter parsing. Use split when want all parts. Use regex for complex patterns. Defensive parsing: handle missing delimiters. Anti-patterns: Manual character-by-character: when built-ins exist. Regex for simple cases: overkill. For the scenario (extract domain): feel domain: substring after(email, "@") ` Simple, direct, idiomatic.
